name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC designs, builds, and launches the world's largest constellation of Earth-imaging satellites, providing daily global imagery at up to 50cm resolution through its SuperDove, SkySat, Pelican, and Tanager hyperspectral platforms. The company delivers geospatial data and analytics via APIs and browser-based tools to agriculture, defense, energy, forestry, finance, and government customers. Founded in 2010 by former NASA scientists, Planet is structured as a Public Benefit Corporation headquartered in San Francisco.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet launched its next-generation Pelican satellite constellation (50cm resolution, 5x improvement over SuperDove) and the Tanager hyperspectral satellite in FY2026, expanding from optical to full-spectrum Earth observation [1]. The Defense & Intelligence segment grew over 50% in FY2026, validating a successful pivot toward high-value government contracts [1]. AI Deployment (2/2): Planet has rebranded as a 'Space and AI' company, deploying machine learning models on its petabyte-scale imagery archive that enable automated change detection, crop classification, and infrastructure monitoring for customers [2]. The company generates quantifiable revenue from AI-powered analytics products integrated into its platform. Market Share (1/2): Planet operates the largest commercial Earth-imaging constellation with 200+ satellites, but faces competition from Maxar, BlackSky, and Airbus Defence & Space; it is a meaningful player but not the undisputed leader in high-resolution imagery [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Pelican constellation deployment continues into FY2027 with specific launch timelines, and FY2027 revenue guidance of $415-$440M (35-43% growth) provides clear forward visibility [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of $106.7M represented 34.7% of FY2026 revenue, well above aerospace & defense industry averages of 3-5% [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's stated mission — 'to accelerate humanity toward a more sustainable, more secure world, by illuminating environmental and social change' — is specific, distinctive, and deeply connected to its satellite technology [3]. As a Public Benefit Corporation, the purpose is legally embedded in governance. Co-founder CEO Will Marshall has articulated this vision consistently since 2010, and strategic decisions (open data initiatives, climate monitoring partnerships) trace back to it. However, the cause has not yet been tested through a major crisis or leadership transition. Community Impact (1/2): Planet has partnered with the Global Heritage Fund to monitor cultural heritage sites in Ukraine, provides imagery to humanitarian organizations, and supports environmental monitoring initiatives, though charitable giving is modest relative to revenue [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): With 945 employees, Planet's Glassdoor reviews describe a mission-driven culture that attracts talented engineers, though reviews also flag challenges with middle management structure and strategic follow-through as the company scales [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Will Marshall maintains significant equity ownership as co-founder, and insider ownership is notable for a company of this size, though no large net insider purchases were identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. FY2026 revenue reached a record $307.7M (up 26% YoY) with gross margin improving to 56.1%, and Q4 revenue of $86.8M beat estimates by 11% [1]. However, Planet remains deeply unprofitable with a -80.2% GAAP profit margin and -$246.9M net loss in FY2026, driven by $161.4M in non-cash charges. Adjusted EBITDA turned positive for the first time at $15.5M. D/E of 245.4% reflects $462.5M total debt against a thin equity base, partially offset by $640M cash [4]. ROE is negative at -78.4%. The 3-year revenue CAGR of 17.2% and strong backlog of $900M+ (up 79% YoY) demonstrate growth momentum, but the absence of GAAP profitability and negative forward P/E prevent meaningful valuation scoring. Scoring: D/E 245%(0.24, 1x) + ROE -78%(0.0, 2x) + RevYoY 26%(1.0, 1x) + Rev5yrCAGR 17%(1.0, 2x) + FwdPE neg(0.0, 1x) + PM -80%(0.0, 1x) + PEG N/A(excluded) = 3.24/8 = 4 [1][4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Planet's core business — designing, launching, and operating 200+ imaging satellites in orbit — involves physical-world space infrastructure that AI cannot replicate or substitute [1]. The company's proprietary daily global imagery dataset spanning 10+ years is a unique moat; no AI model can generate real satellite imagery of actual Earth conditions. Government and defense customers require verified, authoritative geospatial data with chain-of-custody, creating regulatory barriers. However, AI-generated synthetic imagery could theoretically reduce demand for lower-resolution monitoring in some commercial applications, and competitors with AI-enhanced analytics could erode Planet's value-added services layer. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet actively generates revenue from AI-powered analytics, branding itself a 'Space and AI' company [2]. Its machine learning models process petabytes of imagery for automated change detection, crop health monitoring, and infrastructure analysis, with AI capabilities directly embedded in subscription products sold to defense, agriculture, and insurance customers.

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs exhibits strong infinite-game characteristics through its Public Benefit Corporation structure, founder-led mission consistency since 2010, and willingness to invest heavily in long-term satellite technology despite years of losses. The company's Just Cause — illuminating planetary change to build a more sustainable world — is genuine and shapes strategic decisions. However, the company has not yet faced a true existential test, and execution challenges at scale introduce uncertainty about organizational resilience.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Planet's mission to 'accelerate humanity toward a more sustainable, more secure world' is genuine, distinctive, and legally embedded through its PBC structure [3]. Co-founder CEO Will Marshall has held this vision consistently for 16 years since the company's founding by former NASA scientists. Strategic decisions — open data programs, climate monitoring partnerships, choosing daily global coverage over purely commercial optimization — trace back to this purpose. However, the cause has not yet been tested through a leadership transition or major existential crisis, preventing the highest score.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Planet's 945-person workforce is anchored by a core of long-tenured engineers who joined for the mission, and co-founder leadership provides continuity [2]. Glassdoor reviews describe transparency from the executive team and a culture that values 'brilliant and kind' employees. However, reviews also flag inconsistent middle management quality, concerns about strategic follow-through, and scaling challenges that suggest trust varies significantly across teams. The company conducted layoffs in prior years, which impacts the Circle of Safety.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Planet's competitive language focuses on expanding the geospatial market and improving Earth observation capabilities rather than defeating specific competitors [2]. Management acknowledges strengths of rivals like Maxar (higher resolution) and Airbus (established government relationships) while differentiating on daily revisit rate and data accessibility. The company frames competition as motivation to improve its platform rather than a zero-sum battle.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Planet has incrementally evolved from a pure satellite imagery provider to a 'Space and AI' data platform, adding analytics, hyperspectral imaging (Tanager), and higher-resolution capabilities (Pelican) [1]. The Defense & Intelligence pivot (50%+ growth) represents meaningful strategic adaptation. However, the core business model — build satellites, sell imagery — has not been fundamentally reinvented, and the company is still relatively young (founded 2010) with limited existential tests.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Planet has demonstrated courage by sustaining massive R&D investment (34.7% of revenue) through years of losses, choosing to build proprietary satellite manufacturing capability rather than outsourcing, and maintaining its PBC structure despite potential pressure from growth investors [1][3]. The company invested in Tanager hyperspectral technology before clear commercial demand existed. However, the courage has not yet been tested by a situation requiring Planet to walk away from revenue or make a truly painful strategic sacrifice.

investment_thesis: Planet Labs delivered a breakout FY2026 with revenue surging 26% to $307.7M, Q4 revenue of $86.8M beating estimates by 11%, and the company achieving adjusted EBITDA profitability ($15.5M) for the first time [1]. Backlog surged 79% to over $900M with remaining performance obligations up 106% to $852M, providing exceptional forward visibility. The Defense & Intelligence segment grew 50%+, validating Planet's pivot toward high-value government contracts. FY2027 guidance of $415-$440M implies 35-43% top-line growth. With 200+ satellites providing daily global imagery, the Pelican constellation delivering 50cm resolution, and the Tanager hyperspectral platform opening new spectral analysis markets, Planet is building an irreplaceable geospatial data franchise.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on persistent GAAP unprofitability — Planet lost $246.9M in FY2026 (including $161.4M in non-cash charges) with a -80.2% profit margin, and the path to sustainable GAAP profitability remains unclear despite the adjusted EBITDA milestone [1]. D/E of 245.4% reflects significant leverage, and the stock trades at an extreme valuation with no meaningful P/E ratio [4]. Competition from Maxar (Worldview Legion), BlackSky, and Airbus in high-resolution imagery is intensifying. Planet's 945-person workforce may face scaling challenges as revenue accelerates, and Glassdoor reviews flag inconsistent middle management and strategic follow-through concerns [2]. The company's 1,000%+ stock price increase from its 52-week low of $3.47 to $41.59 prices in substantial execution risk.
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue guidance of $415-$440M (35-43% growth) supported by $900M+ backlog and 106% growth in remaining performance obligations, with potential for upside from new Pelican and Tanager contracts
Defense & Intelligence segment momentum with 50%+ growth trajectory as Planet captures expanding government demand for daily persistent surveillance and geospatial AI analytics
Path to GAAP profitability as gross margins expand with higher-margin Pelican and analytics revenue, potentially reaching breakeven by FY2028 as operating leverage improves on a larger revenue base
key_risks: Persistent GAAP losses of $246.9M in FY2026 with -80.2% profit margin — adjusted EBITDA profitability masks continuing cash consumption and dilutive equity financing risk
Extreme valuation after 1,000%+ rally from 52-week lows with no P/E anchor — any miss against $415-$440M FY2027 guidance could trigger severe multiple compression
Intensifying competition from Maxar's Worldview Legion constellation, BlackSky's real-time imaging, and government-built alternatives that could commoditize satellite imagery pricing over time
sources: Planet Labs PBC Q4 FY2026 Earnings Release and FY2027 Guidance — SEC 8-K Filing, March 2026
Planet Labs Company Culture, AI Strategy, and Leadership — Glassdoor Reviews and Company Website, accessed May 2026
Planet Labs PBC ESG Report and Public Benefit Corporation Purpose Statement — planet.com, 2023-2024
